metadata.title: What 81,000 People Want from AI
metadata.color_semantics: claude: INK #3D3929 all text; SPARK #D97757 the one earned accent per beat; mono for code/output only
metadata.description_blurb: Last December, Anthropic pointed an AI interviewer at 80,508 people across 159 countries and asked: if you could wave a magic wand, what would AI do for you? Liam takes the study apart.
metadata.note: claude-liam claude-explainer. Source: Anthropic 'What 81,000 People Want from AI' Mar 2026. LAWS: B00=ClaudeComposerAsk cold open, IN-FOR-BEAR LAW applied. ILLUSTRATE LAW: figures are Remotion scenes. HANDOFF second-to-last. OUTRO restates title.

beats.narration_text: Salaam — this is Liam, in for Bear. Last December, Anthropic pointed an AI interviewer at its own users and asked one

props.command: What do 80,000 people actually want from AI?

beats.narration_text: Eighty thousand five hundred and eight people. A hundred and fifty-nine countries. Seventy languages. Interviewed by an AI — Anthropic Interviewer, a version of Claude itself. This is the largest, most multilingual qualitative study of what people actually want from AI, ever conducted.

What do people hope for? Professional excellence tops the list at eighteen point eight percent. But read the full ranking and the real ask emerges. A third of responses, roughly, are about making room for life — not building a better career, but having time to cook with your mother on a Tuesday. A quarter are about better work. A fifth about becoming someone better.

props.sparkLine: A third want time, not work.

beats.narration_text: A software engineer in Japan put it plainly.

props.quote: I left work on time and picked up my daughter from daycare.

beats.narration_text: Has AI delivered? Eighty-one percent said yes — it has taken a step toward their vision. Productivity dominates at thirty-two percent. But the most affecting stories live in the smallest bar: emotional support, six point one percent. The mute worker who built a text-to-speech bot. The butcher who became an entrepreneur.

props.sparkLine: 81% say yes. But look at the smallest bar.

beats.narration_text: One worker in the dataset sent a hundred and fifty messages over three months — to a text-to-speech bot they built themselves because they cannot speak.

props.quote: I sent over 100–150 messages to you over 3 months.

beats.narration_text: What do people worry about? Unreliability tops the fears at twenty-six point seven percent — that's the only tension where the harm is both anticipated AND experienced. Jobs and economy at twenty-two point three. Autonomy and agency at twenty-one point nine. But here is the finding: jobs-and-economy concern is the single strongest predictor of how someone feels about AI overall.
A freelancer in France described the feeling exactly.

props.quote: You just have to run faster and faster to stay in place.

beats.narration_text: The centerpiece finding: hope and fear don't divide people into camps. They coexist inside the same person. Five tensions, each with a benefit and a harm. And bars split by whether people expect it or have already seen it. Benefits are experienced. Systemic harms are mostly anticipated — a reflection of how early we are. Except unreliability: the one tension where the harm bar is both larger and more lived than the benefit. And emotional support: someone who values it is three times more likely to also fear depending on it. The same person.
An American put the fear plainly.

props.quote: Now people are afraid that they're the horses.

beats.narration_text: Sixty-seven percent net positive globally. No country below sixty percent. But the distribution of concern is not uniform. In Sub-Saharan Africa, eighteen percent reported no concerns at all. In North America, eight percent. The wealthy worry more.

Plot jobs-and-economy concern against negative sentiment and the split is geographic. Wealthier regions cluster top right — higher fear, higher negativity. Developing regions bottom left. Same AI. Different relationship to what it threatens.

There are no AI optimists and no AI pessimists. There are people organized around what they value, holding hope and fear at the same time. The benefits are lived. Most harms are still hypothetical — except unreliability, the one place the dark side is already here. Caveats, stated plainly: these are active Claude users. Early adopters. The interview asked about hopes first, which may shape what followed.

props.artifactTitle: What 81,000 People Want from AI — the verdict
artifactLines: 80,508 people · 159 countries · 70 languages · interviewed by Claude.
Hope #1: professional excellence 18.8% — but a third want time, not work.
81% say AI has taken a step toward their vision. Productivity dominates.
Fear #1: unreliability 26.7% — the only harm that is both big AND lived.
Jobs-&-economy concern is the single strongest predictor of AI sentiment.
Light and shade coexist in the SAME person, not opposing camps.
Caveat: active Claude users, early adopters, hopes asked first.
props.sparkLine: Hope and fear live in the same person.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Over 80,000 people across 159 countries were interviewed by a version of Claude in the largest multilingual study of AI hopes. Professional excellence tops hopes, but a third of responses are really about making room for life. Eighty-one percent said AI took a step toward their vision, with productivity dominating delivery and emotional support the smallest bar. Unreliability tops fears as the only harm both anticipated and experienced, and jobs concern most strongly predicts overall sentiment.

artifactLines: Over 80,000 people across 159 countries were interviewed by a version of Claude in the largest multilingual study of AI hopes.
Professional excellence tops hopes, but a third of responses are really about making room for life.
Eighty-one percent said AI took a step toward their vision, with productivity dominating delivery and emotional support the smallest bar.
Unreliability tops fears as the only harm both anticipated and experienced, and jobs concern most strongly predicts overall sentiment.

beats.narration_text: The finding that jobs-and-economy concern is the single strongest predictor of overall AI sentiment is the thread I want to pull. The study also showed hopes clustering around making room for life, better work, and self-improvement. Can you help me (1) contrast why productivity dominates delivery at thirty-two percent while emotional support sits at just six, (2) explain what it means that unreliability is the only fear both anticipated and experienced, and (3) sketch how I could probe whether that jobs-concern predictor holds within a single country?
